message.abstract:
<jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Frail patients are considered at relevant risk of complications due to COVID-19 infection and, for this reason, are prioritized candidates for vaccination. As these patients were originally not included in the registration trials, fear related to vaccine side-effects and disease worsening was one of the reasons for vaccine hesitancy. Herein we report the safety profile of the prospective, multicenter, national VAX4FRAIL study (
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04848493">NCT04848493</jats:ext-link>
                    ) to evaluate vaccines in a large trans-disease cohort of patients with solid or hematological malignancies, neurological and rheumatological diseases.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Between March 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    and September 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    , 2021, 566 patients were evaluable for safety endpoint: 105 received the mRNA-1273 vaccine and 461 the BNT162b2 vaccine.
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Frail patients were defined per protocol as patients under treatment with hematological malignancies (131), solid tumors (191), immune-rheumatological diseases (86), and neurological diseases (158), including multiple sclerosis and generalized myasthenia.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>The impact of the vaccination on the health status of patients was assessed through a questionnaire focused on the first week after each vaccine dose.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The most frequently reported moderate-severe adverse events were pain at the injection site (60.3% after the first dose, 55.4% after the second), fatigue (30.1% - 41.7%), bone pain (27.4% - 27.2%) and headache (11.8% - 18.9%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Risk factors associated with the occurrence of severe symptoms after vaccine administration were identified through a multivariate logistic regression analysis: age was associated with severe fever presentation (younger patients vs. middle-aged vs. older ones), females presented a higher probability of severe pain at the injection site, fatigue, headache, and bone pain; the mRNA-1237 vaccine was associated with a higher probability of severe pain at the injection site and fever. After the first dose, patients presenting a severe symptom were at a relevant risk of recurrence of the same severe symptom after the second one.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Overall, 11 patients (1.9%) after the first dose and 7 (1.2%) after the second one required to postpone or suspend the disease-specific treatment. Finally, 2 fatal events occurred among our 566 patients. These two events were considered unrelated to the vaccine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study reports that mRNA-COVID-19 vaccination is safe also in frail patients as expected side effects were manageable and had a minimum impact on patient care path.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study reports the safety analysis of the trial VAX4FRAIL confirming that mRNA-COVID-19 vaccination is safe in frail immunocompromised patients: expected side effects were manageable and had a minimum impact on patient care path.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the safety of mRNA-COVID-19 vaccination in vulnerable patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    VAX4FRAIL is a national, multicentric, observational, prospective trial (start date March 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    , 2021 – primary completion date September 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    , 2021).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Multicenter prospective trial.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Frail patients were defined per protocol as patients under treatment with solid tumors (191), immune-rheumatological diseases (86), hematological malignancies (131), and neurological diseases (158), including multiple sclerosis and generalized myasthenia.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Overall, 105 received the mRNA-1273 vaccine and 461 the BNT162b2 vaccine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The occurrence of adverse events after 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    and 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    m-RNA-COVID-19 vaccination was analyzed. Adverse events were collected through a questionnaire comprising both open and closed questions.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The most frequently reported moderate-severe adverse events were pain at the injection site (60.3% after the first dose, 55.4% after the second), fatigue (30.1% - 41.7%), bone pain (27.4% - 27.2%) and headache (11.8% - 18.9%).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Risk factors associated with the occurrence of severe symptoms after vaccine administration were identified through a multivariate logistic regression analysis: age was associated with severe fever presentation (younger patients vs. middle-aged vs. older ones), females presented a higher probability of severe pain at the injection site, fatigue, headache, and bone pain; the mRNA-1237 vaccine was associated with a higher probability of severe pain at the injection site and fever. Patients presenting a severe symptom after the first dose were at a relevant risk of recurrence of the same severe symptom after the second one.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Overall, 11 patients (1.9%) after the first dose and 7 (1.2%) after the second one was required to postpone or suspend their disease-specific treatment. Finally, 2 fatal events occurred among our 566 patients, and these two events were due to disease progression and considered unrelated to the vaccine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study reports that mRNA-COVID-19 vaccination is safe also in frail patients as expected side effects were manageable and had a minimum impact on patient care path.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A National, Multicentric, Observational, Prospective Study to Assess Immune Response to COVID-19 Vaccine in Frail Patients (VAX4FRAIL).
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04848493">NCT04848493</jats:ext-link>
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04848493">https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04848493</jats:ext-link>
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Can m-RNA-COVID19 vaccination be considered safe for frail patients?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      In this national, multicentric, observational, prospective trial (
                      <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04848493">NCT04848493</jats:ext-link>
                      ) that included 566 frail patients, the occurrence of both local and systemic adverse events was manageable and did not negatively impact on the general treatment program.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>mRNA-COVID19 vaccination is safe among frail immunocompromised patients.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: mRNA-COVID19 vaccination can be considered safe and tolerable for frail patients

reference.article-title: Safety of BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine in a cohort of elderly, immunocompromised patients with systemic vasculitis

reference.unstructured: Agrati C , Di Cosimo S , Fenoglio D , Apolone G , Ciceri F , Ciliberto G , et al. COVID-19 Vaccination in Fragile Patients: Current Evidence and an Harmonized Transdisease Trial. Front. Immunol., 10 August 2021 | https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2021.704110.
